data.title: Toronto Stocks Retreat, Restaurant Brands Launches $1 Billion Share Repurchase Program
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks were firmly lower as most sectors were in red territory.

Energy, utilities and tech services were the main stocks ahead in Friday's session, offset by losses primary in consumer discretionary, commercial services and distribution services.

On the broader economic front, Canada's industrial-capacity utilization rate fell by 0.6 percentage points in the second quarter to 79.3%, as forest fires and maintenance work weighed on oil and gas extraction.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell 0.4% to 29302.87 and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 declined by 0.4% to 1731.79.

Canadian building permits edged downward by 0.1% in July because of weakness in demand to build factories, warehouses and public-sector buildings such as hospitals and schools. The value reached a seasonally adjusted 11.86 billion Canadian dollars ($8.57 billion), according to Statistics Canada.

Restaurant Brands International has authorized a share repurchase plan to buy back up to $1 billion of shares over one year. The buyback represents about 10% of its public float. Shares fell 0.5% to C$87.35.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Soybean Futures Slip after WASDE Projects Higher Stocks
data.description:
Soybean futures eased to $10.35 per bushel following the September WASDE on Friday, after hitting a nine-week high of $10.41 earlier in the session.

The report outlined a 2025/26 outlook with higher US production, higher crush, lower exports, and larger ending stocks compared to August. Production was estimated at 4.3 billion bushels, with gains in harvested area offsetting a slight yield reduction to 53.5 bushels per acre.

Crush was raised by 15 million bushels on stronger soybean meal exports, while exports were trimmed by 20 million bushels due to greater competition from Russia, Canada, and Argentina.

Ending stocks rose to 300 million bushels, up 10 million from last month, signaling more comfortable supplies.

The season-average farm price was lowered by 10 cents to $10.00 per bushel, reinforcing a slightly bearish tone for futures despite global adjustments that reduced overall soybean ending stocks.

data.title: USDA Raises Corn Production Outlook to Record
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

The Agriculture Department raised its outlook for how much corn will be produced by U.S. farmers this year, this despite trimming its projection for average corn yields per acre.

In the agency's latest World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates report published Friday, the USDA said that it forecasts 16.81 billion bushels of corn to be produced by farmers in the 2025-26 marketing year-which began at the start of the month. The projection is up from 16.74 billion bushels forecast last month, and remains well above the 2023 record high of 15.34 billion bushels.

Analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal before the report's release had forecast U.S. corn production to slip by over 200 million bushels, to 16.51 billion bushels. The USDA did reduce its projections for corn yields this month, to 186.7 bushels an acre. While surveyed analysts had expected yields to decrease, the USDA didn't trim them as much as expected.

U.S. corn acres are expected to produce an unprecedented amount, with this month's projection down from 188.8 bushels an acre forecast last month, but still far more than the existing record of 179.3 bushels an acre set last year.

In the report, the USDA attributed the higher production to an increase in the amount of corn acres expected to be harvested this year. "If realized, harvested area would be the highest since 1933 and planted area of 98.7 million acres the highest since 1936," the agency said.

The USDA maintained its view that soybean output will be at a record high, though it reduced its yield outlook to 53.5 bushels an acre from 53.6 bushels an acre projected last month. The figure is still much higher than the 51.9 bushels an acre record high set in 2016.

While U.S. corn and soybean production exceeded analyst forecasts, the USDA reduced its outlook for ending stocks of both corn and soybeans globally. World ending stocks for corn are projected at 281.4 million metric tons, and soybeans are seen at 124 million tons. Both are less than expected by surveyed analysts.

Following the release of the report, grain futures trading on the Chicago Board of Trade are mixed. Most-active corn futures are down 0.4%, soybeans are up 0.4% and wheat is down 1%.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Dow Slips After Weaker Sentiment Data — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe and Vicky Ge Huang

A record-breaking week is on track to end on a quieter note.

Stocks were mixed Friday, with the Dow industrials and Nasdaq composite moving in opposite directions. All three major U.S. indexes are on track for gains of over 1% for the week, with the Nasdaq on pace for another record close.

Renewed optimism about the AI boom and confidence in coming interest-rate cuts have helped buoy the market. Traders view a rate cut next week as a near certainty, with a small chance of a bumper half-percentage-point cut, according to CME data based on futures pricing.

Americans' confidence in the economy is waning, while long-term inflation expectations have risen, a preliminary September estimate from the University of Michigan showed Friday. "Consumers perceive risks to their pocketbooks," said Joanne Hsu, director of the surveys.

Shares in Warner Bros. Discovery extended gains after leaping on Thursday on a Wall Street Journal report that Paramount Skydance is preparing a bid.

In recent trading:

Treasury yields rose, with the 10-year yield at around 4.08%.

Oil prices rallied after the U.K. targeted Russia with new sanctions on crude shipments, as well as on defense suppliers.

Gold prices were on pace for a record-high settlement, holding above $3,690 a troy ounce.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: AstraZeneca pauses $270 million investment in Britain
data.description:
Britain's biggest company AstraZeneca has paused a planned 200 million pound ($271.26 million) investment in its Cambridge research site, a spokesperson said, the latest drugmaker to retreat from Britain.

The decision on the investment, which had been set to create 1,000 jobs, means none of the AstraZeneca's planned new funding - originally announced in March 2024 - is currently proceeding.

In January, the company scrapped plans to invest 450 million pounds in its vaccine manufacturing plant in northern England, citing a cut in British government support.

U.S. drugmaker Merck & Co also said this week that it was abandoning a new research centre in London, citing the UK's challenging business environment.

Asked about speculation over its pharmaceutical investments following the Merck announcement, a spokesperson for AstraZeneca, which has the biggest market capitalisation on the FTSE 100, confirmed is would pause its investment plans in Cambridge, where it has one of Britain's leading life sciences hubs.

"We constantly reassess the investment needs of our company and can confirm our expansion in Cambridge is paused. We have no further comment to make," an AstraZeneca spokesperson said.

($1 = 0.7373 pounds)

FAA proposes to fine Boeing $3.1 million over widespread safety violations
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration on Friday proposed fining Boeing $3.1 million for a series of safety violations, including actions tied to the January 2024 Alaska Airlines 737 MAX 9 mid-air emergency, and for interfering with safety officials’ independence.

The FAA found hundreds of quality system violations at the planemaker's 737 factory in Renton, Washington, and Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 factory in Wichita, Kansas.

The FAA also said Boeing presented two unairworthy aircraft to the FAA for airworthiness certificates and failed to follow its quality system rules.

The FAA found that a Boeing employee pressured another Boeing worker performing tasks on behalf of the FAA to sign off on a Boeing 737 MAX airplane so the company could meet its delivery schedule despite the fact the employee had determined the aircraft did not comply with standards.

Boeing and Spirit AeroSystems did not immediately comment.

In June, the National Transportation Safety Board said Boeing failed to provide adequate training, guidance, and oversight to prevent the Alaska Airlines mid-air cabin panel blowout that spun the planemaker into a major crisis.

The board harshly criticized Boeing's safety culture and its failure to install four key bolts in a MAX 9 during production, as well as ineffective oversight by the FAA.

Earlier this week, FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford told Reuters the agency had not made any decision on whether to lift the 38 planes per month 737 MAX production cap in place since early 2024, or on how it oversees Boeing production.

The FAA continues to inspect each 737 MAX and 787 aircraft before an airworthiness certificate is issued and cleared for delivery. Typically, the FAA delegates airplane ticketing authority to the manufacturer.

data.title: The Week in Oil: Market Pulled Between Supply Glut Worries, Geopolitical Risks
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

Here's a look at what happened in oil markets in the week of Sept. 8-12 and what the focus will be in the days to come.

OVERVIEW: Oil prices have fluctuated between gains and losses this week amid concerns over a looming supply surplus and rising geopolitical risks. In evening trade in Europe, Brent crude and West Texas Intermediate hovered around $67 and $63 a barrel, respectively. Both benchmarks are on track for weekly gains of around 3% after the U.K. unveiled new sanctions targeting Russia's oil flows and a Ukrainian drone attack on a Russian port reportedly forced the suspension of loadings.

MACRO: The macroeconomic outlook is bullish for oil, with the Federal Reserve expected to cut interest rates by 25 basis points next week. Lower rates make borrowing cheaper, encouraging economic activity and typically leading to higher demand for crude.

The labor market weakness is fueling dovish bets, with jobless claims hitting the highest level since October 2021. Consumer prices continued to rise last month--above economists' expectations--but they did little to alter market expectations of imminent cuts.

GEOPOLITICAL RISKS: Heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and Eastern Europe provided underlying support to prices this week, despite bearish fundamentals.

Israel attacked Hamas's leadership in Qatar, complicating regional dynamics. Meanwhile, Russian supplies have been under the spotlight amid intensifying Ukrainian strikes on key infrastructure and prospects of tougher Western sanctions.

"Fundamentals have so far been unaffected by moves to target Russia's oil exports due to reorienting supply chain, sanctions evasion and, more recently, India's willingness to continue importing Russian crude despite higher U.S. tariffs," said Kieran Tompkins from Capital Economics.

However, pressure from Western nations is intensifying.

The U.K. unveiled a fresh package of 100 sanctions targeting ships carrying Russian oil and companies supplying electronics, chemicals and explosives used to manufacture weapons.

Meanwhile, the EU is currently preparing a 19th package of sanctions against Russia and reportedly considering a faster phase-out of Russian fossil fuels. Also, two EU officials said President Trump asked the bloc to hit some goods from China and India--two major buyers of Russian oil--with tariffs of up to 100%.

SUPPLY AND DEMAND: Persistent concerns over a looming supply surplus are putting pressure on prices, with the International Energy agency now projecting higher supply growth this year and next.

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries instead left its oil-demand forecasts unchanged after agreeing to raise production again next month, doubling down on its strategy shift in a push for market share.

"While OPEC+ has slowed the pace of production hikes compared with earlier months, its focus on reclaiming market share continues to pressure sentiment," analysts at MUFG said earlier this week.

Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories also rose for a second straight week, underscoring softer demand in the world's largest consumer. "The latest EIA [Energy Information Administration] snapshot showed a sizeable crude build alongside very high refinery utilization-evidence that domestic supply remains ample even with peak summer runs now ebbing," said Felipe Barragan, strategist at Pepperstone. "Elevated stocks blunt the price impact of geopolitical scares and make rallies harder to sustain without a clean demand impulse."

NEXT WEEK: For the time being, oil prices are unlikely to move significantly, as the drag from increased OPEC+ production is countered by persistent geopolitical tensions, market watchers said.

Traders will continue to closely monitor geopolitical developments, with media reports saying the U.S. plans to urge its Group of Seven allies to impose tariffs on China and India over their purchases of Russian oil.

The September Fed meeting kicks off next week, with a policy decision expected on Wednesday. "Assuming the Fed does cut, markets will pay close attention to the updated statement and press conference with Powell to see whether this is the first of several cuts or if the guidance is more guarded around inflation risks," Saxo analysts said.

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com

data.title: LA Kings and Blue Shield of California Unite for 9/11 Day Meal Pack To Support Families Facing Food Insecurity
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / September 12, 2025 / In recognition of the September 11 National Day of Service and Remembrance, AEG's LA Kings joined forces with Blue Shield of California to participate in 9/11 Day's Meal Pack Program in Los Angeles, an initiative that honors those lost on 9/11 by giving back to the local community.

On Wednesday, September 11, volunteers from the LA Kings and Blue Shield of California gathered at the LAFD Frank Hotchkin Memorial Training Center to assemble meal kits for families in need. Joining more than 1,200 volunteers, the group helped pack 450,000 meals, which will be distributed by the Los Angeles Regional Food Bank to help those experiencing hunger across Southern California.

Launched in the aftermath of the September 11 attacks, 9/11 Day has become the nation's largest annual day of service. This year's Meal Pack program spanned 21 cities and mobilized tens of thousands of volunteers nationwide to prepare more than nine million meals for vulnerable communities - continuing the legacy of unity and compassion that defined the days following 9/11.

"The LA Kings are committed to serving our community and honoring the resilience that September 11 represents," said Amanda Apel, Senior Director of Community Relations, LA Kings. "Through our G.O.A.L.S. program, we are proud to partner with Blue Shield of California and 9/11 Day to provide meals to families who need them most. Coming together in service reminds us that acts of kindness-big and small-have the power to create lasting change."

The date also holds a personal significance for the Kings organization. On September 11, 2001, two members of the team's scouting staff, Garnet "Ace" Bailey and Mark Bavis, were among those who tragically lost their lives aboard United Airlines Flight 175, which was flown into the South Tower of the World Trade Center. Their memory continues to inspire the team's community work and commitment to service.

Through initiatives like the Meal Pack program, 9/11 Day encourages people of all ages to pay tribute to the victims, survivors, and first responders by engaging in acts of service that strengthen communities and spread compassion.

Volunteers from the LA Kings and Blue Shield of California gathered at the LAFD Frank Hotchkin Memorial Training Center to assemble meal kits for families in need.

Market Purchase by the Company of its own Shares

Notification is given that, pursuant to the authority granted at the General Meeting of the Company held on 1 July 2025 to make market purchases of the Company's own ordinary shares up to a maximum of 14.99%, a market purchase of 50,000 ordinary shares in the capital of the Company at a price of 847.6577p per share was made on 12 September 2025. The purchased shares will be held in Treasury. 

Following the above purchase, the Company's issued ordinary share capital continues to be 74,385,131 ordinary shares of 25p each, of which 10,560,854 shares are held in Treasury and have no voting rights.

Holders of the Company's ordinary shares are entitled to one vote for every share held. As Treasury shares have no voting rights, the total number of voting rights is 63,824,277. There are also 4,257 preference stock units in issue, which do not carry voting rights.

data.title: Adobe Earnings Were 'Solid.' Long-Term Growth Concerns Remain. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Adobe stock was down slightly Friday as some analysts celebrated the latest earnings results, while others still have doubts about the creative software company's long term growth opportunities.

Adobe reported better-than-expected third-quarter financial results after the stock market closed on Thursday. Earnings and revenue beat analyst estimates, annualized recurring revenue was stronger than expected, and artificial intelligence influenced revenue surpassed the company's goals. Adobe also raised its full year earnings and revenue guidance.

"Adobe reported solid third-quarter results that outperformed consensus estimates on all key metrics," William Blair analyst Jake Roberge wrote in a research note Friday.

Roberge believes that the Adobe is "well positioned to capitalize on the GenAI opportunity, the increasing need for creative content, and shifting consumer preferences toward digital interactions." He rates Adobe as Outperform without a price target.

A highlight from the report was signs that demand for the company's AI software has been growing. Adobe announced that 99% of Fortune 100 companies have used AI in an Adobe app, and over 40% of its top 50 enterprise accounts doubled their annualized recurring revenue spend since the start of fiscal year 2023.

"We view the strength in the AI marketing suite, GenStudio, as validation that Adobe's value proposition for leveraging AI across both content creation and workflow is resonating in the enterprise," BofA Securities analyst Brad Sills wrote in a note Thursday night.

Despite maintaining a Buy rating for Adobe, Sills cut his price target on the stock to $460 from $475, citing multiple compression for software applications stocks this year.

Adobe currently trades at 15.3 times earnings expected over the next 12 months, down from 21.5 times on Jan. 1 of this year. Another software stock, ServiceNow, trades at 49 times forward earnings, below 63.8 times on Jan. 1, while Salesforce trades at 20 times forward earnings, below the 30 times multiple at the start of the year.

Deutsche Bank analyst Brad Zelnick wrote in a research note Thursday night that even though "results were an incremental positive, we don't expect the event to meaningfully impact longer-term debates on the stock."

"We still expect the stock to remain rangebound until the company demonstrates more tangible success from AI in its overall financials," Zelnick wrote. He rates Adobe as a Hold with a $405 price target.

Shares of Adobe were down 0.6% Thursday to $348.39. The stock has dropped 21% this year.
